original_abstract:
Large-scale relational learning is essential for managing the vast quantities of structured data generated across domains such as computational biology, information retrieval, and natural language processing. In this paper, we introduce a novel neural network architecture designed to embed multi-relational graphs into a flexible, continuous vector space. This approach preserves and enhances the underlying structure of the original data. By training the network to capture the latent semantics of these graphs, the model learns to assign high probabilities to plausible relational components.

Our empirical evaluation demonstrates that this method achieves competitive performance in link prediction tasks on standard benchmarks and real-world knowledge bases like WordNet. Furthermore, we illustrate the scalability and versatility of our approach by applying it to word-sense disambiguation within an open-text semantic parsing framework. In this setting, the model learns to assign structured meaning representations to nearly any free-text sentence, proving it can effectively scale to datasets containing tens of thousands of nodes and thousands of unique relation types.
humanized_abstract:
Data released in structured formats across many domains such as computational biology, information retrieval, and natural language processing is in huge supply. As such, large-scale relational learning is critical to data analysis. In this paper, we propose a neural network model, OpenNE-ML, that could project multi-relational graphs to a continuous vector space with flexible properties, and maintained the intrinsic structure. More importantly, OpenNE-ML could train the model to learn the latent semantic connections that would output the high probability of the corresponding relational components.

Our experimental evaluation shows that our approach performs remarkably well in link prediction tasks over popular datasets and a sample of real world knowledge bases such as WordNet. Finally, we also showcase the scalability and applicability of our model by demonstrating its performance in a specific instantiation of the word-sense disambiguation task, and we demonstrate how our model can be seamlessly integrated into an open-text semantic parsing framework, which is able to assign a structured semantic parse to virtually any free text sentence. This larger experiment allows us to truly measure the scalability of our model by providing experiments on datasets of tens of thousands of examples and thousands of relations.

document.output:
Data released in structured formats across many domains such as computational biology, information retrieval, and natural language processing is in huge supply. As such, large-scale relational learning is critical to data analysis. In this paper, we propose a neural network model, OpenNE-ML, that could project multi-relational graphs to a continuous vector space with flexible properties, and maintained the intrinsic structure. More importantly, OpenNE-ML could train the model to learn the latent semantic connections that would output the high probability of the corresponding relational components.

Our experimental evaluation shows that our approach performs remarkably well in link prediction tasks over popular datasets and a sample of real world knowledge bases such as WordNet. Finally, we also showcase the scalability and applicability of our model by demonstrating its performance in a specific instantiation of the word-sense disambiguation task, and we demonstrate how our model can be seamlessly integrated into an open-text semantic parsing framework, which is able to assign a structured semantic parse to virtually any free text sentence. This larger experiment allows us to truly measure the scalability of our model by providing experiments on datasets of tens of thousands of examples and thousands of relations.
document.input:
Large-scale relational learning is essential for managing the vast quantities of structured data generated across domains such as computational biology, information retrieval, and natural language processing. In this paper, we introduce a novel neural network architecture designed to embed multi-relational graphs into a flexible, continuous vector space. This approach preserves and enhances the underlying structure of the original data. By training the network to capture the latent semantics of these graphs, the model learns to assign high probabilities to plausible relational components.

Our empirical evaluation demonstrates that this method achieves competitive performance in link prediction tasks on standard benchmarks and real-world knowledge bases like WordNet. In this setting, the model learns to assign structured meaning representations to nearly any free-text sentence, proving it can effectively scale to datasets containing tens of thousands of nodes and thousands of unique relation types.
